concept.domains: (topic concept) (topic abbrev-d) a(props deliveryTarget) (topic equation-d) (topic hazard-d) (topic hi-d) (topic indexing-d) (topic markup-d) (topic mathml-d) (topic pr-d) (topic relmgmt-d) (topic sw-d) (topic svg-d) (topic ui-d) (topic ut-d) (topic markup-d xml-d)
concept.title: Understanding the workflow step language

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Learn when to use markdown-validator workflows instead of individual rules, and how the step language expresses conditional rule dependencies.

concept.shortdesc: Workflows chain rules together with conditional logic. Use them when a rule should only run if another rule passes or fails — for example, "check H2 structure only when the document is a tutorial." Simple checks that are always evaluated do not need workflows.

section.title: When to use workflows

ul.li: A check is only meaningful if another check passes first. For example: the H1 must start with "Tutorial:" only applies when ms.topic == tutorial.
You need to express conditional presence. For example: "if there are no H2 headings, then there should also be no H3 headings."
Two checks together constitute a single editorial requirement. For example: either the Prerequisites heading exists, or the article is marked as a reference — but not both.

section.p: Do not use a workflow for checks that are always evaluated independently. Keep the rule list simple; add workflows only when the conditional logic is genuinely needed.

section.title: The workflow as a directed graph
section.p: Before reading the step token syntax, build this mental model: a workflow is a small directed graph. The nodes in the graph are:

dlentry.dd: The fixed entry point. Every workflow begins here.
Each rule referenced in the workflow is a node. Execution passes through these nodes and each one produces a pass or fail result.
A branching node. The result of the rule just before D determines which branch runs next. A passing result takes the True (T) branch; a failing result takes the False (F) branch.
A convergence node. Both branches of a decision point lead back to M so the workflow has a single exit path.
The fixed exit point. Every workflow ends here.

section.p: The steps field encodes the edges of this graph as a comma-separated list of source-target tokens. Each token is one directed edge: "execution can travel from source to target."

section.title: The simplest workflow: a single rule
section.p: Before reading the full token syntax, see what the simplest possible workflow looks like. A linear, unconditional check uses just two tokens:

fig.title: Graph of the simple workflow S-1,1-E

image.alt: Flowchart: S leads directly to Rule 1, which leads directly to End. No decision or merge nodes are needed.

section.p: S-1 means "Start leads to Rule 1." 1-E means "Rule 1 leads to End." There is no decision node because the result is always the result of Rule 1. This is the pattern to start with and add complexity only when conditional logic is required.

section.title: Reading step tokens for conditional workflows
section.p: When a workflow needs a conditional branch, it uses the Decision (D) and Merge (M) nodes. Here is the token string for a conditional workflow and the graph it produces:

fig.title: Graph of the conditional workflow S-1,1-D,T-2,F-3,2-M,3-M,M-E

image.alt: Flowchart: S leads to Rule 1 (precondition). Rule 1 leads to D (decision node). The true branch from D leads to Rule 2. The false branch leads to Rule 3. Both Rule 2 and Rule 3 lead to M (merge). M leads to End. Edge labels show the token that encodes each transition.

row.entry: Edge from Start to Rule 1. Rule 1 runs first.
Edge from Rule 1 to the Decision node. Rule 1's pass/fail result controls the branch.
True branch: if Rule 1 passed, run Rule 2.
False branch: if Rule 1 failed, run Rule 3.
Edge from Rule 2 to the Merge node.
Edge from Rule 3 to the Merge node.
Edge from Merge to End. The workflow is complete.

section.title: Build a workflow from a plain-English requirement
section.p: The easiest way to write a workflow token string is to build it one edge at a time, starting from the requirement in plain English.
Requirement: "Tutorial articles must have an H1 that starts with 'Tutorial:'. Other article types are not checked for H1 format."

Step 1 — Identify the nodes. This requirement has a precondition (is this a tutorial?) and a conditional check (only for tutorials). That means the graph needs: S, a precondition rule, D, a check rule for the True branch, a sentinel rule for the False branch (see step 3), M, and E.
Assign IDs: Rule 1 checks ms.topic == tutorial. Rule 2 checks the H1 starts with "Tutorial:". Rule 10 is a sentinel (a rule that is known to always pass for any valid document).

p.b: Step 2 — Draw the edges one at a time.

row.entry: Start → Rule 1 (precondition: is this a tutorial?)
Rule 1 → Decision (its result controls the branch)
True branch → Rule 2 (check H1 prefix for tutorials)
False branch → Rule 10 (sentinel; skip H1 check)

section.p: Step 3 — Why the sentinel rule? The F-M token does not exist. Every branch must pass through a rule node before reaching M. When the False branch represents "nothing to check here," route it through a sentinel — a rule whose precondition always passes for well-formed documents. The sentinel contributes a pass result and the branch continues cleanly to M.

section.title: Practical example: enforcing different H1 formats per topic type
section.p: When both branches of a decision carry meaningful checks — not just "skip" — no sentinel is needed. This workflow enforces a different H1 prefix depending on ms.topic:

ul.li: If ms.topic == tutorial (Rule 1 passes), the H1 must start with "Tutorial:" (Rule 2).
If ms.topic != tutorial (Rule 1 fails), the H1 must start with "How to" (Rule 3).

section.p: Rule 1 is the precondition; D routes to Rule 2 or Rule 3 depending on the topic type; both paths merge before the workflow ends. Because both branches run a real check, no sentinel is needed.

section.title: Avoid the workflow-inversion pattern
section.note: The pattern described below is a known design smell. Do not use it for new rules. It exists only as a workaround until the negate flag is available in v0.3.
section.p: Some teams use workflow branching to express "this thing must NOT exist" — for example, "the title must not contain the word 'guide'." Because markdown-validator has no negation operator in v0.2, this requires inverting the pass/fail result by routing branches in reverse: the True branch (content IS present — the bad state) loops back into a failing rule, and the False branch (content is absent — the good state) routes to a sentinel that passes.

This pattern is fragile. A single miswiring of T and F branches causes the rule to silently pass bad content or fail good content — and the error is difficult to spot by reading the token string alone. If you must use it, test it exhaustively and document the inversion clearly in your rule set's README. For the full pattern and its known failure modes, see .
When v0.3 ships, replace any inverted workflows with a standard rule that uses "negate": true.

ul.li: For the full step-pattern reference, see .
